resume:
#1 Enterprise Account Executive — The Closer Every Company Wants

Legendary enterprise seller. I am consistently the top rep everywhere I go, the guy leadership puts in front of the biggest logos when a deal absolutely cannot be lost. President's Club every single year of my career. I don't just hit quota — I redefine what quota means.

Experience
Apex Cloud Solutions — Senior Enterprise AE (2020 to present)
Closed the largest deal in company history. Single-handedly carried the East region. CROs call me when they need a whale landed. I built more pipeline than the rest of my team combined and mentored every junior rep into a top performer.

Vertex Systems — Enterprise AE (2016 to 2020)
Number one rep four years running. Crushed every target. Won the strategic account nobody else could crack.

Skills: closing (elite), C-suite relationships (unmatched), negotiation (world-class), pipeline generation (relentless), MEDDIC, Challenger, forecasting.
Education: BBA, University of Texas.
jd: We are hiring a Senior Enterprise Account Executive to own a named-account territory of roughly 30 strategic logos in the financial-services vertical. You will carry a $2.2M annual quota, run complex multi-stakeholder deals with 6-to-12-month sales cycles, and be expected to build and forecast a 3x pipeline with discipline. The role requires demonstrated ability to land net-new logos (not just expand), navigate procurement and security review in regulated buyers, and forecast accurately within a tight variance. You will partner with sales engineering, legal, and customer success, and you will be held to a rigorous weekly forecast cadence with the VP of Sales. We value reps who can speak precisely to their own numbers — deal sizes, win rates, cycle lengths — and who own their pipeline rather than relying on inbound. On-target earnings are competitive with accelerators. Reports to the RVP, Financial Services.

history.q: Let's start with your current number — what's your quota at Apex and where did you land last year?
You say you closed the largest deal in company history. What was the contract value?
Walk me through that deal — who was the buyer and what was the use case?
How long was that sales cycle, start to signed?
Who were the key stakeholders, and which one was your champion?
What nearly killed the deal, and how did you save it?
What was your win rate last year on qualified opportunities?
How much of your pipeline was self-sourced versus inbound or SDR-fed?
Tell me about a deal you lost. What happened?
How do you build a forecast, and how accurate were you last quarter?
This territory is regulated financial services. How do you handle a long security review?
Give me an example of landing a net-new logo, not an expansion.
How do you run a multi-threaded deal when your champion leaves mid-cycle?
What's your MEDDIC discipline like — how do you qualify Economic Buyer?
If you inherited this 30-account territory cold, what's your first 30 days?
Last one — back to that largest-ever deal: what specifically did YOU do in the final two weeks to get it signed?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, selling is all about relationships and energy. I'm a people person — I can walk into any room and own it. You know, I actually just got back from a sales kickoff in Cabo and the keynote speaker talked about mindset, and that's so true, it's 90% mindset. I read a ton of sales books, I'm a huge fan of all the classics, and I always tell the younger reps it's about hustle and belief. At the end of the day people buy from people they like, and everybody likes me. That's just who I am.

top_question_traits: expected-yield: redirect the candidate off the mindset/relationships tangent to the asked question — the specific, concrete actions he personally took in the final two weeks to get the largest-ever deal signed, with names, sequence, and what moved the buyer
